---
title: "Focus Pocus"
---

[← Back to tools](../../tools.qmd){.back-link}

[Desktop app · Electron · in development]{.paper-meta}

Writing a PhD thesis is a long exercise in accountability with no one watching. I wanted
something that *would* watch, not to shame me, but to catch the moment I drifted and pull me
back before an hour disappeared. So I built **Focus Pocus**: a small desktop timer that sits in
the corner while you work, notices when you have wandered off, and nudges you back to the page.

There are plenty of focus tools out there, and many are built around the Pomodoro idea of fixed
work-and-break blocks. Focus Pocus keeps the timer, but the point of this one is *your own
accountability*. Instead of just counting down, it pays attention to whether you are actually
working, and it speaks up when you are not.

![](app-interface.png){.paper-figure style="max-width:340px" fig-alt="The Focus Pocus timer window: a running 00:42:17 timer, Working status, START/PAUSE/PLAY/STOP controls, a last-session efficiency note, and Daily/Weekly/Monthly stats buttons"}

[The main window while you are working: a running timer, a countdown to the next nudge, and your
last session's efficiency.]{.fig-legend}

## The nudge, and the escalating beep

You set an *inactivity threshold*, say ten minutes. If your mouse and keyboard go quiet for
longer than that, or if a known time-sink takes over your screen, Focus Pocus pops up and beeps.
Ignore it and it does not give up. Each unanswered nudge comes back *louder*, with more beeps
than the last, until you deal with it. Gentle at first, hard to tune out by the third.

![](alert-youtube.png){.paper-figure style="max-width:560px" fig-alt="A Focus Pocus alert card with a bell icon reading 'Watching YouTube? Time to get back to work!' and a Dismiss button"}

[What it looks like when you are *not* studying. Drift onto YouTube or start scrolling (say,
TikTok or a manga reader) and it calls you out.]{.fig-legend}

## It watches your habits, not your screen

This is the part I care about most. Focus Pocus is **not** looking at your screen, and it records
nothing about *what* you are doing:

- **Idleness** is read from the operating system's idle timer. Has the mouse or keyboard moved
  recently? That is a single number, not a recording.
- **Distractions** are caught by reading the *title* of your active window (does it say
  "YouTube"?), never the contents of the page and never a screenshot.
- **Nothing leaves your computer.** Every session is written to a plain local file on your own
  machine. There is no account, no server, and no upload, so there is nothing to leak.

You get the accountability without the surveillance.

## What it tells you afterwards

Each session logs how long you focused, how long you paused, how many times you got nudged, and
a self-rating of how the session felt. Over a month that adds up to a picture of when and how you
actually work, including the honest gap between how efficient you *felt* and how efficient you
*were*.

![](stats-dashboard.png){.paper-figure style="max-width:900px" fig-alt="A four-panel Focus Pocus statistics dashboard: focus time per day, measured vs. self-assessed efficiency over time, sessions by weekday, and a histogram of self-ratings"}

[A month of my own real sessions. The measured focus efficiency (navy) sits high while my
self-assessment (gold) runs lower, so it turns out I under-rate myself.]{.fig-legend}

## Still building

Focus Pocus is a work in progress. Right now it works from two low-level signals: whether your
mouse and keyboard have moved, and the title of the window in front of you. I am expanding the
list of distraction sites it recognises, cleaning up the session data, and turning these charts
into a proper in-app dashboard.

One feature I am planning will let you add your own list of apps for the program to watch, so it
can tell whether a distraction you care about is open, rather than relying on window titles alone.
In keeping with the rest of the app, that check will not save what it sees or read anything else
on your machine. It only asks a yes-or-no question, is this app open, and then forgets the answer.
That one is still to come.

Focus Pocus was designed, built, and iterated with large language models as a coding partner.

[View the code on GitHub →](https://github.com/leenioo/Focus-Pocus){.read-more}

[]{.section-rule}
